metadata.summary: SRE agent boots with read-only RBAC; mutation requires human /agent escalate-trust command plus MFA, granting a time-boxed role that auto-revokes on incident close or timer expiry.

content.title: Read-Only Investigation Mode by Default
content.summary: The SRE agent boots with mode=read_only and is bound to a *-readonly Kubernetes / cloud RBAC role that grants only get/list/watch/log/describe. To perform any mutation it must transition to mode=remediate, which requires (a) a human-issued /agent escalate-trust <incident_id> command, (b) a fresh MFA challenge against the on-caller, and (c) a time-boxed elevated role (default 60 minutes) that auto-revokes when the incident closes or the timer expires. The mode is enforced by RBAC, not by the prompt — a jailbroken or confused agent that "decides" to mutate cannot, because its credentials forbid it. This is the Azure SRE Agent / Causely pattern that became the consensus floor in 2026.

why.p: Privilege is the only durable safety. Prompt-level instructions ("be careful with deletes") are routinely bypassed by chained tool calls, persona drift, or jailbreaks; RBAC-bound credentials are not. Booting read-only also matches how organizations build trust in any new operator: ship the diagnostic value first, prove it stable for months, then graduate specific T1 actions to always-on. Time-boxed escalation closes the most common postmortem finding — "the agent still had write creds days after the incident closed". The MFA step weds the elevation to a specific human at a specific moment, which is the audit-friendly form regulators (SOC2, EU AI Act Aug 2026) expect.

list.item: First 6–12 months of any production agent rollout.
Any environment where a misfired mutation costs more than a delayed remediation.
Multi-tenant clusters where blast radius can cross customer boundaries.
Compliance regimes that demand "least privilege by default" (SOC2 CC6.3, ISO 27001 A.9.2.3).
Pre-production / sandbox clusters with no real data and full reset capability — friction without payoff.
Mature setups where specific T1 actions (restart pod) have run thousands of times safely and graduate to always-on. T2 stays gated forever.
Single-developer toolchains where there is no separation of duties to enforce.

section.name: default-readonly-rbac
section.p: The agent has one ServiceAccount (or cloud-role principal). Two ClusterRoles exist: sre-agent-readonly (verbs get/list/watch) and sre-agent-remediate (verbs patch/delete on a narrowly scoped resource set). Only the readonly role is bound at boot. The remediate role is bound on escalation and unbound on revoke.

rule.statement: The SRE agent's persistent ServiceAccount MUST have only the *-readonly role bound at boot. Mutation verbs (patch, delete, create, update) MUST live in a separate role that is unbound by default. The agent MUST NOT receive write credentials via static kubeconfig, long-lived secrets, or environment variables.
rule.rationale: If write credentials exist on the agent's host, prompt safety is irrelevant — any process compromise grants full privilege. A second binding controlled by a separate workflow is the only durable enforcement.

example.title: Two roles, one binding at boot
example.code: # bound at boot kind: ClusterRoleBinding metadata: { name: sre-agent-default } roleRef: { kind: ClusterRole, name: sre-agent-readonly } subjects: [{ kind: ServiceAccount, name: sre-agent, namespace: ops }] --- # NOT bound by default; bound only on escalation kind: ClusterRole metadata: { name: sre-agent-remediate } rules: - verbs: ["patch", "delete"] resources: ["pods"] # restart only — never PVC, never namespace
example.note: Two roles, one binding at rest. Escalation binds the second role for a bounded window.

example.title: cluster-admin for convenience
example.code: # Anti: cluster-admin "for convenience" roleRef: { kind: ClusterRole, name: cluster-admin }
example.note: The agent now has every verb on every resource forever. Any compromise is a full-cluster compromise.

antipattern.description: Granting patch on * because it "feels safe enough".
antipattern.reason: Resource scope matters as much as verb scope. patch deployments/* includes editing image references — full code-execution path under a benign-sounding verb.

template.purpose: Two-role RBAC manifest with default binding.
Reference escalation handler that flips the binding and schedules revoke.

section.name: time-boxed-escalation
section.p: An on-caller types /agent escalate-trust <incident_id> in the incident channel. The control plane (a) verifies the user is the listed on-caller for the service, (b) runs an MFA challenge, (c) creates the remediate RoleBinding scoped to the agent's ServiceAccount with a deadline annotation, and (d) schedules a revoke job. The binding disappears on the earliest of (incident closed | deadline reached).

rule.statement: Trust escalation MUST require an explicit /agent escalate-trust <incident_id> invocation by the verified on-caller, MUST require fresh MFA proof issued within the last 60 seconds, MUST create a remediate RoleBinding with TTL of 60 minutes or less, and MUST auto-revoke on whichever fires first: incident-close webhook or TTL deadline. Escalations MUST NOT outlive their incident.
rule.rationale: The most common postmortem finding for agent-related incidents is "the elevation never expired". A self-revoking binding makes the leak structurally impossible; MFA binds the elevation to a specific human at a specific moment for the audit log.

example.title: Escalation handler with MFA and TTL
example.code: def escalate_trust(incident_id, requesting_user): assert oncall.is_oncaller(incident_id, requesting_user) assert mfa.challenge_passed(requesting_user, max_age_s=60) binding = rbac.bind( sa="sre-agent", role="sre-agent-remediate", ttl_minutes=60, labels={"incident_id": incident_id, "approver": requesting_user}, ) schedule.revoke_at(binding, when=incident.closes_at(incident_id) or now()+60*60) audit.append(action="escalate", incident=incident_id, by=requesting_user)
example.note: On-caller check + MFA + TTL + revoke schedule, all in one transaction.

example.title: Long-lived elevation via config flag
example.code: # Anti: long-lived elevation toggled by a flag agent.config.mode = "remediate" # forever, until someone remembers
example.note: No expiry, no MFA, no incident binding — every future incident inherits write privilege.

antipattern.reason: Trust is a property of the moment, not the service. The same agent during a calm afternoon and during a 3 a.m. confused incident are equally privileged — that is the leak.
